-# Workflow Quick Reference
-
-**Model:** Contractor (you direct, Claude orchestrates)
-
----
-
-## The Loop
-
-```
-Your instruction

-[PLAN] (if multi-file or unclear) â†’ Show plan â†’ Your approval

-[EXECUTE] Implement, verify, done

-
----
-
-## I Ask You When
-
-- **Design forks:** "Option A (fast) vs. Option B (robust). Which?"
-- **Code ambiguity:** "Spec unclear on X. Assume Y?"
-- **Replication edge case:** "Just missed tolerance. Investigate?"
-- **Scope question:** "Also refactor Y while here, or focus on X?"
-

-
-## I Just Execute When
-
-- Code fix is obvious (bug, pattern application)
-- Verification (tolerance checks, tests, compilation)
-- Documentation (logs, commits)
-- Plotting (per established standards)
-- Deployment (after you approve, I ship automatically)
-

-

-

-|-------|--------|

-

0â†’# Skill Creation Template
     1â†’
     2â†’**Use this template to create domain-specific skills for your academic workflow.**
     3â†’
     4â†’---
     5â†’
     6â†’## When to Create a Custom Skill
     7â†’
     8â†’Create a skill when you find yourself:
     9â†’- Repeatedly explaining the same 3+ step workflow to Claude
    10â†’- Needing domain-specific quality checks (citation style, notation consistency, lab protocols)
    11â†’- Enforcing field-specific output formats (thesis structure, journal templates, lab notebooks)
    12â†’- Coordinating multi-tool workflows (Figma â†’ R â†’ LaTeX, data â†’ analysis â†’ manuscript)
    13â†’
    14â†’**Don't create a skill for:**
    15â†’- One-time tasks
    16â†’- Workflows that change frequently
    17â†’- Simple 1-2 step operations
    18â†’
    19â†’---
    20â†’
    21â†’## Template Structure
    22â†’
    23â†’Copy the structure below to `.claude/skills/[your-skill-name]/SKILL.md`:
    24â†’
    25â†’```markdown
    26â†’---
    27â†’name: your-skill-name
    28â†’description: [What it does] + [When to use it] + [Key capabilities]. Use when user asks for "[trigger phrase 1]", "[trigger phrase 2]", or "[context]".
    29â†’argument-hint: "[brief hint for user]"
    30â†’allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write", "Edit", "Grep", "Glob", "Bash", "Task"]
    31â†’disable-model-invocation: true
    32â†’---
    33â†’
    34â†’# [Skill Name]
    35â†’
    36â†’[One sentence: what this skill accomplishes and why it exists]
    37â†’
    38â†’## Instructions
    39â†’
    40â†’Step 1: [First major action with clear explanation]
    41â†’   - Detail: [Important consideration]
    42â†’   - Example: [Concrete example]
    43â†’
    44â†’Step 2: [Second major action]
    45â†’   - Detail: [Important consideration]
    46â†’
    47â†’Step 3: [Final action and verification]
    48â†’   - Verify: [What to check]
    49â†’   - Output: [What user receives]
    50â†’
    51â†’## Examples
    52â†’
    53â†’### Example 1: [Common Scenario Name]
    54â†’**Context:** [When this occurs]
    55â†’**User says:** "[Typical user request]"
    56â†’**Actions:**
    57â†’1. [What skill does first]
    58â†’2. [What skill does second]
    59â†’3. [Final output]
    60â†’**Result:** [What user receives]
    61â†’
    62â†’### Example 2: [Another Common Scenario]
    63â†’**Context:** [Different situation]
    64â†’**User says:** "[Alternative phrasing]"
    65â†’**Actions:**
    66â†’1. [Different path through the skill]
    67â†’**Result:** [Expected outcome]
    68â†’
    69â†’## Troubleshooting
    70â†’
    71â†’**Error:** [Common error message or symptom]
    72â†’**Cause:** [Why this happens]
    73â†’**Solution:** [How to fix it]
    74â†’
    75â†’**Error:** [Another common issue]
    76â†’**Cause:** [Root cause]
    77â†’**Solution:** [Fix steps]
    78â†’```
    79â†’
    80â†’---
    81â†’
    82â†’## Writing Effective Descriptions
    83â†’
    84â†’The `description` field determines when Claude loads your skill. Use this structure:
    85â†’
    86â†’```
    87â†’[What it does] + [When to use it] + [Key capabilities]
    88â†’```
    89â†’
    90â†’### Good Examples (Field-Specific)
    91â†’
    92â†’**Citation Style Enforcement (APA Psychology):**
    93â†’```yaml
    94â†’description: Enforces APA 7th edition citation format in manuscripts. Use when user asks to "check citations", "fix references", "apply APA style", or when reviewing .tex or .qmd files with bibliography. Checks author-year format, DOI formatting, and reference list completeness.
    95â†’```
    96â†’
    97â†’**Lab Notebook Entry Generator (Wet Lab Biology):**
    98â†’```yaml
    99â†’description: Generates structured lab notebook entries from experimental notes. Use when user provides "experiment notes", "protocol results", or asks to "format lab entry". Ensures date, hypothesis, materials, procedure, observations, and conclusions are documented.
   100â†’```
   101â†’
   102â†’**Thesis Chapter Structure Checker (Graduate Students):**
   103â†’```yaml
   104â†’description: Validates thesis chapter structure against institutional requirements. Use when user asks to "check chapter format", "validate thesis structure", or when editing thesis .tex or .docx files. Verifies required sections, heading levels, and citation density.
   105â†’```
   106â†’
   107â†’**Econometric Specification Review (Economics):**
   108â†’```yaml
   109â†’description: Reviews econometric specifications for common errors. Use when user shares regression code in R or Stata, or asks to "check model spec", "review estimation". Validates: standard error clustering, fixed effects structure, missing covariates, and replication commands.
   110â†’```
   111â†’
   112â†’### Bad Examples (Too Generic)
   113â†’
   114â†’â‌Œ `description: Helps with citations`
   115â†’â†’ Too vague, doesn't specify style or when to trigger
   116â†’
   117â†’â‌Œ `description: Checks lab notebooks`
   118â†’â†’ Missing trigger phrases, no format specification
   119â†’
   120â†’â‌Œ `description: Reviews thesis chapters`
   121â†’â†’ Doesn't specify what aspects or when to activate
   122â†’
   123â†’---
   124â†’
   125â†’## Academic Domain Examples
   126â†’
   127â†’### Example 1: Citation Cross-Reference Checker
   128â†’
   129â†’**File:** `.claude/skills/validate-citations/SKILL.md`
   130â†’
   131â†’```markdown
   132â†’---
   133â†’name: validate-citations
   134â†’description: Cross-references in-text citations against bibliography entries. Use when user asks to "check citations", "validate references", or when working on .tex, .qmd, or .md files with bibliographies. Identifies missing entries, unused references, and formatting inconsistencies.
   135â†’argument-hint: "[file or 'all']"
   136â†’allowed-tools: ["Read", "Grep", "Glob", "Write"]
   137â†’---
   138â†’
   139â†’# Validate Citations
   140â†’
   141â†’Ensures all in-text citations have corresponding bibliography entries and identifies unused references.
   142â†’
   143â†’## Instructions
   144â†’
   145â†’Step 1: **Extract in-text citations**
   146â†’   - Use Grep to find citation commands: `\cite{...}`, `\citep{...}`, `[@...]`
   147â†’   - Build list of cited keys
   148â†’
   149â†’Step 2: **Parse bibliography file**
   150â†’   - Read Bibliography_base.bib (or specified .bib file)
   151â†’   - Extract all `@article{key,`, `@book{key,` entries
   152â†’
   153â†’Step 3: **Cross-reference**
   154â†’   - Missing in bib: citations without entries
   155â†’   - Unused in bib: entries never cited
   156â†’
   157â†’Step 4: **Report findings**
   158â†’   - Save to `quality_reports/citation_validation.md`
   159â†’   - List missing entries (CRITICAL)
   160â†’   - List unused entries (for cleanup)
   161â†’   - Check DOI formatting if present
   162â†’
   163â†’## Examples
   164â†’
   165â†’### Example 1: Manuscript with Missing Citation
   166â†’**User says:** "Check if all my citations are in the bib file"
   167â†’**Actions:**
   168â†’1. Grep for `\citep{` in manuscript.tex
   169â†’2. Extract keys: `Smith2020`, `Jones2019`, `Lee2021`
   170â†’3. Parse Bibliography_base.bib
   171â†’4. Find `Lee2021` missing
   172â†’**Result:** Report: "Missing entry: Lee2021. Add to bibliography before submission."
   173â†’
   174â†’## Troubleshooting
   175â†’
   176â†’**Error:** Citation format not recognized
   177â†’**Cause:** Non-standard citation command
   178â†’**Solution:** Add pattern to Step 1 grep (e.g., `\citeauthor`, `\citeyear`)
   179â†’```
   180â†’
   181â†’---
   182â†’
   183â†’### Example 2: Regression Output Formatter
   184â†’
   185â†’**File:** `.claude/skills/format-regression-tables/SKILL.md`
   186â†’
   187â†’```markdown
   188â†’---
   189â†’name: format-regression-tables
   190â†’description: Converts R regression outputs to publication-ready LaTeX tables. Use when user runs regressions and says "make a table", "format results", or "export to LaTeX". Handles lm, glm, felm, and fixest objects. Applies field-specific conventions (standard errors in parentheses, stars for significance).
   191â†’argument-hint: "[model object name]"
   192â†’allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write", "Bash"]
   193â†’---
   194â†’
   195â†’# Format Regression Tables
   196â†’
   197â†’Converts R regression objects into publication-ready LaTeX tables with proper formatting.
   198â†’
   199â†’## Instructions
   200â†’
   201â†’Step 1: **Identify model objects**
   202â†’   - User provides R object names (e.g., `model1`, `model2`)
   203â†’   - Read corresponding .rds files from output/
   204â†’
   205â†’Step 2: **Extract coefficients and statistics**
   206â†’   - Coefficient estimates
   207â†’   - Standard errors (in parentheses)
   208â†’   - Significance stars: * p<0.10, ** p<0.05, *** p<0.01
   209â†’   - R-squared, N observations
   210â†’
   211â†’Step 3: **Generate LaTeX table**
   212â†’   - Use booktabs style (no vertical lines)
   213â†’   - Align on decimal points
   214â†’   - Add notes for standard errors and significance
   215â†’
   216â†’Step 4: **Save and verify**
   217â†’   - Save to `output/tables/regression_results.tex`
   218â†’   - Verify compiles in standalone .tex document
   219â†’
   220â†’## Examples
   221â†’
   222â†’### Example 1: Panel Regression with Fixed Effects
   223â†’**User says:** "Format my regression results for the paper"
   224â†’**Context:** User has run `felm(y ~ x | firm + year | 0 | firm, data=df)`
   225â†’**Actions:**
   226â†’1. Read saved model object
   227â†’2. Extract: coefficients, clustered SEs, Rآ², N
   228â†’3. Generate LaTeX:
   229â†’   ```latex
   230â†’   \begin{table}[htbp]
   231â†’   \caption{Effect of X on Y}
   232â†’   \begin{tabular}{lc}
   233â†’   \toprule
   234â†’   & (1) \\
   235â†’   \midrule
   236â†’   X & 0.523*** \\
   237â†’     & (0.089) \\
   238â†’   \midrule
   239â†’   Firm FE & Yes \\
   240â†’   Year FE & Yes \\
   241â†’   \midrule
   242â†’   Observations & 1,234 \\
   243â†’   R-squared & 0.678 \\
   244â†’   \bottomrule
   245â†’   \multicolumn{2}{l}{\footnotesize Clustered SEs in parentheses.} \\
   246â†’   \multicolumn{2}{l}{\footnotesize * p<0.10, ** p<0.05, *** p<0.01}
   247â†’   \end{tabular}
   248â†’   \end{table}
   249â†’   ```
   250â†’**Result:** Ready to `\input` in manuscript.tex
   251â†’
   252â†’## Troubleshooting
   253â†’
   254â†’**Error:** Model object not found
   255â†’**Cause:** .rds file not in expected location
   256â†’**Solution:** Check output/ directory, verify saveRDS() was called
   257â†’
   258â†’**Error:** Standard errors missing
   259â†’**Cause:** Model didn't specify clustering/robust SEs
   260â†’**Solution:** Re-run with vcov specification, document assumption
   261â†’```
   262â†’
   263â†’---
   264â†’
   265â†’### Example 3: Experimental Protocol Validator
   266â†’
   267â†’**File:** `.claude/skills/validate-protocol/SKILL.md`
   268â†’
   269â†’```markdown
   270â†’---
   271â†’name: validate-protocol
   272â†’description: Validates experimental protocols against lab safety and reproducibility standards. Use when user provides lab protocol documents, asks to "check protocol", "validate procedure", or when reviewing .md or .docx experimental designs. Ensures required sections, safety notes, and replication details are present.
   273â†’argument-hint: "[protocol file]"
   274â†’allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write"]
   275â†’---
   276â†’
   277â†’# Validate Experimental Protocol
   278â†’
   279â†’Checks experimental protocols for completeness, safety documentation, and reproducibility.
   280â†’
   281â†’## Instructions
   282â†’
   283â†’Step 1: **Parse protocol document**
   284â†’   - Extract sections: Objective, Materials, Procedure, Safety, Expected Results
   285â†’   - Check for required metadata: Date, Version, Author
   286â†’
   287â†’Step 2: **Validate required elements**
   288â†’   - Materials list complete (catalog numbers, suppliers)
   289â†’   - Safety warnings for hazardous materials
   290â†’   - Quantitative measurements (volumes, temperatures, times)
   291â†’   - Controls specified
   292â†’
   293â†’Step 3: **Reproducibility check**
   294â†’   - Equipment specifications listed
   295â†’   - Randomization described (if applicable)
   296â†’   - Sample sizes justified
   297â†’   - Data recording method specified
   298â†’
   299â†’Step 4: **Generate report**
   300â†’   - PASS/FAIL for each section
   301â†’   - Missing elements highlighted
   302â†’   - Suggestions for improvement
   303â†’
   304â†’## Examples
   305â†’
   306â†’### Example 1: Cell Culture Protocol
   307â†’**User says:** "Check if my protocol is complete before submitting to IRB"
   308â†’**Context:** Protocol for isolating primary neurons
   309â†’**Actions:**
   310â†’1. Read protocol.md
   311â†’2. Check Materials: âœ“ catalog numbers present
   312â†’3. Check Safety: âœ— missing biosafety level designation
   313â†’4. Check Procedure: âœ“ quantitative (37آ°C, 5% COâ‚‚, 10 mL media)
   314â†’**Result:** Report: "Missing: BSL-2 designation. Add before IRB submission."
   315â†’
   316â†’## Troubleshooting
   317â†’
   318â†’**Error:** Cannot parse sections
   319â†’**Cause:** Non-standard heading format
   320â†’**Solution:** Add heading patterns to Step 1 (e.g., "### Safety Notes")
   321â†’```
   322â†’
   323â†’---
   324â†’
   325â†’## Testing Your Skill
   326â†’
   327â†’### Step 1: Manual Test
   328â†’1. Create skill directory: `mkdir -p .claude/skills/your-skill-name`
   329â†’2. Copy SKILL.md template and customize
   330â†’3. Restart Claude Code or run `/reload` if available
   331â†’4. Trigger skill: Use one of your trigger phrases
   332â†’5. Verify: Skill loads, instructions are clear, output is correct
   333â†’
   334â†’### Step 2: Iteration
   335â†’- **If skill doesn't trigger:** Revise description with more specific trigger phrases
   336â†’- **If instructions unclear:** Add more examples and detail to Steps
   337â†’- **If output wrong:** Add validation steps, troubleshooting section
   338â†’
   339â†’### Step 3: Success Criteria
   340â†’- âœ… Skill triggers on 90%+ of relevant queries
   341â†’- âœ… Complete workflow in expected number of steps
   342â†’- âœ… Zero API errors during normal operation
   343â†’- âœ… Same task yields consistent outputs across sessions
   344â†’
   345â†’---
   346â†’
   347â†’## Field-Specific Customization Checklist
   348â†’
   349â†’When adapting this template to your domain:
   350â†’
   351â†’- [ ] Replace example trigger phrases with your field's terminology
   352â†’- [ ] Add domain-specific file types (`.R`, `.py`, `.ipynb`, `.tex`, `.stan`)
   353â†’- [ ] Include field conventions (notation, formatting, citation styles)
   354â†’- [ ] Reference standard tools (`ggplot2`, `pandas`, `TikZ`, `Stata`)
   355â†’- [ ] Add common error messages from your toolchain
   356â†’- [ ] Include institutional requirements (thesis formats, journal templates)
   357â†’
   358â†’---
   359â†’
   360â†’## Allowed Tools Reference
   361â†’
   362â†’| Tool | Use For |
   363â†’|------|---------|
   364â†’| `Read` | Reading file contents (scripts, manuscripts, data) |
   365â†’| `Write` | Creating new files (reports, tables, outputs) |
   366â†’| `Edit` | Modifying existing files in place |
   367â†’| `Grep` | Searching file contents (citations, function names) |
   368â†’| `Glob` | Finding files by pattern (*.R, *.tex, *.csv) |
   369â†’| `Bash` | Running commands (R scripts, LaTeX compilation, git) |
   370â†’| `Task` | Launching subagents (for complex multi-step workflows) |
   371â†’
   372â†’**Security note:** Only grant `Bash` access if your skill needs to execute code or compile documents. For read-only validation skills, omit it.
   373â†’
   374â†’---
   375â†’
   376â†’## Where This Template Lives
   377â†’
   378â†’- **File:** `templates/skill-template.md`
   379â†’- **Purpose:** Starter for domain-specific skills
   380â†’- **Usage:** Copy to `.claude/skills/[name]/SKILL.md`, customize for your field
   381â†’

# Skill Creation Template

**Use this template to create domain-specific skills for your academic workflow.**

---

## When to Create a Custom Skill

Create a skill when you find yourself:
- Repeatedly explaining the same 3+ step workflow to Claude
- Needing domain-specific quality checks (citation style, notation consistency, lab protocols)
- Enforcing field-specific output formats (thesis structure, journal templates, lab notebooks)
- Coordinating multi-tool workflows (Figma â†’ R â†’ LaTeX, data â†’ analysis â†’ manuscript)

**Don't create a skill for:**
- One-time tasks
- Workflows that change frequently
- Simple 1-2 step operations

---

## Template Structure

Copy the structure below to `.claude/skills/[your-skill-name]/SKILL.md`:

```markdown
---
name: your-skill-name
description: [What it does] + [When to use it] + [Key capabilities]. Use when user asks for "[trigger phrase 1]", "[trigger phrase 2]", or "[context]".
argument-hint: "[brief hint for user]"
allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write", "Edit", "Grep", "Glob", "Bash", "Task"]
disable-model-invocation: true
---

# [Skill Name]

[One sentence: what this skill accomplishes and why it exists]

## Instructions

Step 1: [First major action with clear explanation]
   - Detail: [Important consideration]
   - Example: [Concrete example]

Step 2: [Second major action]
   - Detail: [Important consideration]

Step 3: [Final action and verification]
   - Verify: [What to check]
   - Output: [What user receives]

## Examples

## Writing Effective Descriptions

The `description` field determines when Claude loads your skill. Use this structure:

```
[What it does] + [When to use it] + [Key capabilities]
```

### Good Examples (Field-Specific)

**Citation Style Enforcement (APA Psychology):**
```yaml
description: Enforces APA 7th edition citation format in manuscripts. Use when user asks to "check citations", "fix references", "apply APA style", or when reviewing .tex or .qmd files with bibliography. Checks author-year format, DOI formatting, and reference list completeness.
```

**Lab Notebook Entry Generator (Wet Lab Biology):**
```yaml
description: Generates structured lab notebook entries from experimental notes. Use when user provides "experiment notes", "protocol results", or asks to "format lab entry". Ensures date, hypothesis, materials, procedure, observations, and conclusions are documented.
```

**Thesis Chapter Structure Checker (Graduate Students):**
```yaml
description: Validates thesis chapter structure against institutional requirements. Use when user asks to "check chapter format", "validate thesis structure", or when editing thesis .tex or .docx files. Verifies required sections, heading levels, and citation density.
```

**Econometric Specification Review (Economics):**
```yaml
description: Reviews econometric specifications for common errors. Use when user shares regression code in R or Stata, or asks to "check model spec", "review estimation". Validates: standard error clustering, fixed effects structure, missing covariates, and replication commands.
```

### Bad Examples (Too Generic)

â‌Œ `description: Helps with citations`
â†’ Too vague, doesn't specify style or when to trigger

â‌Œ `description: Checks lab notebooks`
â†’ Missing trigger phrases, no format specification

â‌Œ `description: Reviews thesis chapters`

name: validate-citations
description: Cross-references in-text citations against bibliography entries. Use when user asks to "check citations", "validate references", or when working on .tex, .qmd, or .md files with bibliographies. Identifies missing entries, unused references, and formatting inconsistencies.
argument-hint: "[file or 'all']"
allowed-tools: ["Read", "Grep", "Glob", "Write"]

# Validate Citations

Ensures all in-text citations have corresponding bibliography entries and identifies unused references.

## Instructions

Step 1: **Extract in-text citations**
   - Use Grep to find citation commands: `\cite{...}`, `\citep{...}`, `[@...]`
   - Build list of cited keys

Step 2: **Parse bibliography file**
   - Read Bibliography_base.bib (or specified .bib file)
   - Extract all `@article{key,`, `@book{key,` entries

Step 3: **Cross-reference**
   - Missing in bib: citations without entries
   - Unused in bib: entries never cited

Step 4: **Report findings**
   - Save to `quality_reports/citation_validation.md`
   - List missing entries (CRITICAL)
   - List unused entries (for cleanup)
   - Check DOI formatting if present

## Examples

### Example 1: Manuscript with Missing Citation
**User says:** "Check if all my citations are in the bib file"
**Actions:**
1. Grep for `\citep{` in manuscript.tex
2. Extract keys: `Smith2020`, `Jones2019`, `Lee2021`
3. Parse Bibliography_base.bib
4. Find `Lee2021` missing
**Result:** Report: "Missing entry: Lee2021. Add to bibliography before submission."

## Troubleshooting

**Error:** Citation format not recognized
**Cause:** Non-standard citation command
**Solution:** Add pattern to Step 1 grep (e.g., `\citeauthor`, `\citeyear`)
```

name: format-regression-tables
description: Converts R regression outputs to publication-ready LaTeX tables. Use when user runs regressions and says "make a table", "format results", or "export to LaTeX". Handles lm, glm, felm, and fixest objects. Applies field-specific conventions (standard errors in parentheses, stars for significance).
argument-hint: "[model object name]"
allowed-tools: ["Read", "Write", "Bash"]
---

# Format Regression Tables

Converts R regression objects into publication-ready LaTeX tables with proper formatting.

## Instructions

Step 1: **Identify model objects**
   - User provides R object names (e.g., `model1`, `model2`)
   - Read corresponding .rds files from output/

Step 2: **Extract coefficients and statistics**
   - Coefficient estimates
   - Standard errors (in parentheses)
   - Significance stars: * p<0.10, ** p<0.05, *** p<0.01
   - R-squared, N observations

Step 3: **Generate LaTeX table**
   - Use booktabs style (no vertical lines)
   - Align on decimal points
   - Add notes for standard errors and significance

Step 4: **Save and verify**
   - Save to `output/tables/regression_results.tex`
   - Verify compiles in standalone .tex document

## Examples

### Example 1: Panel Regression with Fixed Effects
**User says:** "Format my regression results for the paper"
**Context:** User has run `felm(y ~ x | firm + year | 0 | firm, data=df)`
**Actions:**
1. Read saved model object
2. Extract: coefficients, clustered SEs, Rآ², N
3. Generate LaTeX:
   ```latex
   \begin{table}[htbp]
   \caption{Effect of X on Y}
   \begin{tabular}{lc}
   \toprule
   & (1) \\
   \midrule
   X & 0.523*** \\
     & (0.089) \\
   \midrule
   Firm FE & Yes \\
   Year FE & Yes \\
   \midrule
   Observations & 1,234 \\
   R-squared & 0.678 \\
   \bottomrule
   \multicolumn{2}{l}{\footnotesize Clustered SEs in parentheses.} \\
   \multicolumn{2}{l}{\footnotesize * p<0.10, ** p<0.05, *** p<0.01}
   \end{tabular}
   \end{table}
   ```
**Result:** Ready to `\input` in manuscript.tex

## Troubleshooting

**Error:** Model object not found
**Cause:** .rds file not in expected location
**Solution:** Check output/ directory, verify saveRDS() was called

**Error:** Standard errors missing
**Cause:** Model didn't specify clustering/robust SEs
**Solution:** Re-run with vcov specification, document assumption
```

# Validate Experimental Protocol

Checks experimental protocols for completeness, safety documentation, and reproducibility.

## Instructions

Step 1: **Parse protocol document**

Step 2: **Validate required elements**
   - Materials list complete (catalog numbers, suppliers)
   - Safety warnings for hazardous materials
   - Quantitative measurements (volumes, temperatures, times)
   - Controls specified

Step 3: **Reproducibility check**
   - Equipment specifications listed
   - Randomization described (if applicable)
   - Sample sizes justified
   - Data recording method specified

Step 4: **Generate report**

   - Missing elements highlighted
   - Suggestions for improvement

## Examples

**Actions:**

2. Check Materials: âœ“ catalog numbers present

4. Check Procedure: âœ“ quantitative (37آ°C, 5% COâ‚‚, 10 mL media)
**Result:** Report: "Missing: BSL-2 designation. Add before IRB submission."

## Troubleshooting

**Error:** Cannot parse sections
**Cause:** Non-standard heading format
**Solution:** Add heading patterns to Step 1 (e.g., "### Safety Notes")

# Project Memory

Corrections and learned facts that persist across sessions.
When a mistake is corrected, append a `[LEARN:category]` entry below.

---

<!-- Append new entries below. Most recent at bottom. -->

## Workflow Patterns

[LEARN:workflow] Requirements specification phase catches ambiguity before planning â†’ reduces rework 30-50%. Use spec-then-plan for complex/ambiguous tasks (>1 hour or >3 files).

[LEARN:workflow] Spec-then-plan protocol: AskUserQuestion (3-5 questions) â†’ create `quality_reports/specs/YYYY-MM-DD_description.md` with MUST/SHOULD/MAY requirements â†’ declare clarity status (CLEAR/ASSUMED/BLOCKED) â†’ get approval â†’ then draft plan.

[LEARN:workflow] Context survival before compression: (1) Update MEMORY.md with [LEARN] entries, (2) Ensure session log current (last 10 min), (3) Active plan saved to disk, (4) Open questions documented. The pre-compact hook displays checklist.

[LEARN:workflow] Plans, specs, and session logs must live on disk (not just in conversation) to survive compression and session boundaries. Quality reports only at merge time.

## Documentation Standards

[LEARN:documentation] When adding new features, update BOTH README and guide immediately to prevent documentation drift. Stale docs break user trust.

[LEARN:documentation] Always document new templates in README's "What's Included" section with purpose description. Template inventory must be complete and accurate.

[LEARN:documentation] Guide must be generic (framework-oriented) not prescriptive. Provide templates with examples for multiple workflows (LaTeX, R, Python, Jupyter), let users customize. No "thou shalt" rules.

[LEARN:documentation] Date fields in frontmatter and README must reflect latest significant changes. Users check dates to assess currency.

## Design Philosophy

[LEARN:design] Framework-oriented > Prescriptive rules. Constitutional governance works as a TEMPLATE with examples users customize to their domain. Same for requirements specs.

[LEARN:design] Quality standard for guide additions: useful + pedagogically strong + drives usage + leaves great impression + improves upon starting fresh + no redundancy + not slow. All 7 criteria must hold.

[LEARN:design] Generic means working for any academic workflow: pure LaTeX (no Quarto), pure R (no LaTeX), Python/Jupyter, any domain (not just econometrics). Test recommendations across use cases.

## File Organization

[LEARN:files] Specifications go in `quality_reports/specs/YYYY-MM-DD_description.md`, not scattered in root or other directories. Maintains structure.

[LEARN:files] Templates belong in `templates/` directory with descriptive names. Currently have: session-log.md, quality-report.md, exploration-readme.md, archive-readme.md, requirements-spec.md, constitutional-governance.md.

## Constitutional Governance

[LEARN:governance] Constitutional articles distinguish immutable principles (non-negotiable for quality/reproducibility) from flexible user preferences. Keep to 3-7 articles max.

[LEARN:governance] Example articles: Primary Artifact (which file is authoritative), Plan-First Threshold (when to plan), Quality Gate (minimum score), Verification Standard (what must pass), File Organization (where files live).

[LEARN:governance] Amendment process: Ask user if deviating from article is "amending Article X (permanent)" or "overriding for this task (one-time exception)". Preserves institutional memory.

## Skill Creation

[LEARN:skills] Effective skill descriptions use trigger phrases users actually say: "check citations", "format results", "validate protocol" â†’ Claude knows when to load skill.

[LEARN:skills] Skills need 3 sections minimum: Instructions (step-by-step), Examples (concrete scenarios), Troubleshooting (common errors) â†’ users can debug independently.

[LEARN:skills] Domain-specific examples beat generic ones: citation checker (psychology), protocol validator (biology), regression formatter (economics) â†’ shows adaptability.

## Memory System

[LEARN:memory] Two-tier memory solves template vs working project tension: MEMORY.md (generic patterns, committed), personal-memory.md (machine-specific, gitignored) â†’ cross-machine sync + local privacy.

[LEARN:memory] Post-merge hooks prompt reflection, don't auto-append â†’ user maintains control while building habit.

## Meta-Governance

[LEARN:meta] Repository dual nature requires explicit governance: what's generic (commit) vs specific (gitignore) â†’ prevents template pollution.

[LEARN:meta] Dogfooding principles must be enforced: plan-first, spec-then-plan, quality gates, session logs â†’ we follow our own guide.

[LEARN:meta] Template development work (building infrastructure, docs) doesn't create session logs in quality_reports/ â†’ those are for user work (slides, analysis), not meta-work. Keeps template clean for users who fork.
